home.title: Home
home.subtitle: A community-written backlog for open-source learning
intro: LikeAnOpenSource is being built as a community-written library of project notes, articles, and guides.
Right now the most honest thing the site can do is show what should be written next and make that backlog easy to contribute to through GitHub.

callout.text: If you want to help shape the catalog, start in Contribute. If you want to understand the editorial direction first, start with Explore.

groups.title: How the Platform Works
intro: The current version is intentionally honest about what exists and what is still waiting for real contributors.

items.title: Editorial direction first
items.description: Explore explains what the platform wants to surface once real community content starts landing.

items.title: Repository notes are still open work
items.description: Projects now defines what a good repository note should cover instead of pretending a finished catalog already exists.

items.title: Long-form writing will be community-written
items.description: Articles now describes the writing standard and points readers to open article topics rather than seeded essays.

items.title: Guide quality before guide volume
items.description: Guides explains what future step-by-step paths should do before the first guide library is published.

items.title: The backlog lives in GitHub
items.description: Open topics are repository-backed briefs. When a topic is added or removed in GitHub, the site reflects that change automatically.

items.label: For contributors
items.text: Open Contribute and choose a topic brief that matches the format you want to write.

items.label: For editors
items.text: Use Explore, Projects, Articles, and Guides to check whether the backlog and empty states still describe the product honestly.

items.label: For curious readers
items.text: Read Explore first, then jump into the section whose future content you care most about.

items: Clarity matters more than polish. A page should tell visitors what is real today and what still depends on future contributors.
A smaller, honest backlog is more useful than a larger fake catalog. The site should never imply that community-written content already exists when it does not.

blocks.text: No section should simulate momentum. Content earns visibility only after a real contributor writes it and a real reviewer accepts it.

explore.title: Explore
explore.subtitle: What LAOS wants to surface once the community library starts growing
intro: Explore is not a featured feed yet. It is a public statement about the kind of material this platform wants to earn over time.
For now it explains what deserves space on the site and sends contributors toward the backlog that can make that future real.

items: A useful discovery layer does more than list popular repositories. It helps someone understand why a thing matters, how difficult it is to adopt, and where to go next.
Until LAOS has real community-written notes and articles, Explore should describe that editorial intent clearly instead of pretending the feed is already full.

items.label: Clear onboarding
items.text: A reader should understand what the project or topic is, how to start, and what effort it asks from them.

items.label: Visible maintenance
items.text: A useful piece should talk about stewardship, issue hygiene, release rhythm, or other signals of repository health.

items.label: Transferable learning
items.text: The content should teach something reusable instead of just praising a tool.

items.label: Honest tradeoffs
items.text: If something is powerful but costly, niche, or difficult, the writing should say so directly.

items.label: Actionable next step
items.text: Good discovery content reduces hesitation by making the next click or next contribution obvious.

blocks.text: Nothing is featured here until a real contributor has written it and a real reviewer has accepted it.

groups.title: What Needs To Be Written Next

items.title: Repository notes we still need
items.description: Projects explains what a repository note must cover and sends contributors to the open backlog.

items.title: Article topics waiting for real writers
items.description: Articles defines the editorial bar and points to the article briefs that still need to be written.

items.title: Guide briefs waiting for contributors
items.description: Guides explains the future guide model and links into the open guide backlog.

items.title: Browse the full backlog
items.description: Contribute is the single source of truth for open topic briefs that can be added or removed through GitHub.

projects.title: Projects
projects.subtitle: Repository notes will appear here once contributors publish real evaluations
intro: There are no published community-written project notes yet.
This section now defines what a strong repository note should do and sends contributors toward the backlog instead of pretending the catalog already exists.

items: The public project catalog is intentionally empty until someone writes a repository note grounded in a real project, real evidence, and real editorial review.
When the first accepted project note is added to the repository, it can appear here automatically through the same GitHub-backed content model.

blocks.text: No repository note is published until it has been written by a contributor and merged through GitHub review.

groups.title: Published Repository Notes
intro: Accepted repository notes will appear in this section automatically once real records are merged into `content/projects/index.json`.
Until then, this heading stays visible as an honest placeholder for the first published notes instead of a fake catalog.

groups.title: What A Project Note Must Cover

items.label: Why the repository matters
items.text: Explain the problem the repository solves and who it helps.

items.label: Onboarding path
items.text: Show what a newcomer sees in the README, setup flow, contribution docs, and first useful issue path.

items.label: Maintenance signal
items.text: Describe release rhythm, issue hygiene, documentation quality, or other visible trust signals.

items.label: Learning value
items.text: Clarify what a reader can learn from this repository beyond a simple feature list.

items.label: Contribution reality
items.text: Be honest about how easy or difficult it is for someone to contribute usefully.

items.title: Browse open project topics
items.description: Open the backlog and look for briefs tagged `Project Topic` if you want to write the first repository notes for LAOS.

items.title: See how topics become published notes
items.description: The Contribute section explains how a topic moves from backlog brief to accepted public content through GitHub review.

articles.title: Articles
articles.subtitle: Long-form articles will appear here after contributors publish real writing
intro: There are no published community-written articles yet.
Instead of showing seeded essays, this section now defines the editorial standard and points contributors toward the open article backlog.

items: The article library stays empty until someone writes a real piece with a clear reader, a real question, and defensible reasoning.
Once a reviewed article record is merged into the repository, it can appear here automatically without hand-authoring new HTML.

blocks.text: Articles should publish judgment, not filler. If a piece cannot help a reader make a better technical decision, it does not belong here yet.

groups.title: Published Articles
intro: Reviewed long-form writing will appear here automatically once accepted article records are added to `content/articles/index.json`.
For now this remains a truthful empty library rather than a seeded reading room.

groups.title: What A Good Article Must Do

items.label: Name the reader
items.text: Every article should make its intended audience obvious from the start.

items.label: Explain the tradeoff
items.text: The writing should show what a decision buys, what it costs, and when it stops being the right choice.

items.label: Ground the argument
items.text: Use practical workflow evidence, repository signals, or real team consequences instead of vague praise.

items.label: Stay reusable
items.text: A strong article teaches an evaluation lens that a reader can use again elsewhere.

items.title: Browse open article topics
items.description: Open the backlog and look for briefs tagged `Article Topic` if you want to write the first long-form LAOS pieces.

items.title: Understand the publish path
items.description: The Contribute workflow explains how an article brief becomes a reviewed public article through GitHub.

guides.title: Guides
guides.subtitle: Guide contribution model and writing rules
intro: No full guide library is published yet.
Until that exists, this section explains the kind of guide paths LAOS wants and what contributors should optimize for when writing them.

items: A useful guide should move someone from uncertainty to action. It should tell them where to begin, what to compare, and how to decide that they are ready for the next step.
That means the future guide library should be built around decision paths, not around generic topic dumping or overly polished summaries.

items.title: Browse open guide topics
items.description: Open the backlog and look for briefs tagged `Guide Topic` if you want to write the first guide paths for LAOS.

items.title: See how guide briefs move forward
items.description: The Contribute workflow explains how a guide brief becomes real published guide content through GitHub review.

contribute.title: Contribute
contribute.subtitle: GitHub-backed topic backlog for future community-written content
intro: Every topic in this section is a real request for future content.
If a topic disappears from the repository, it disappears from the site. If a new topic is merged, it shows up here automatically.

callout.text: There is no submission form yet. GitHub pull requests are the only source of truth for topic visibility and published content.

items: The backlog is the place where LAOS makes its editorial needs visible. It tells contributors what kind of project notes, articles, and guides the platform still needs next.
This backlog is intentionally repository-backed. That keeps visibility simple: the site shows only what the repository currently asks for.

groups.title: Topic Briefs
intro: Open a brief before you start writing. Each brief explains the intended reader, the expected shape, and the kinds of evidence the final piece should include.

items.label: 1. Choose a brief
items.text: Pick a topic from the backlog that matches the format you want to write.

items.label: 2. Work in the repository
items.text: Create or update the relevant JSON and Markdown files inside the repo. The site itself does not author content.

items.label: 3. Open a focused pull request
items.text: Explain what topic you addressed, what content you added, and why it meets the brief.

items.label: 4. Pass review
items.text: Moderation happens through GitHub review. A topic becomes public content only after a reviewer accepts the work.

items.label: 5. Let the site update
items.text: Once merged, the new topic or published content shows up automatically through the repo-backed fetch flow.

blocks.text: Backlog visibility and published content both follow the same rule: if it is not in the repository, it is not on the site.

groups.title: What A Good Contribution Must Do

items.label: Be specific
items.text: Name the reader, the problem, and the real decision the content is trying to improve.

items.label: Stay honest
items.text: Do not imply firsthand knowledge, contributor friendliness, or maintenance quality without showing why.

items.label: Reduce hesitation
items.text: A strong piece should help the next person feel less lost and more ready to act.

items.label: Fit the format
items.text: Project notes, articles, and guides serve different jobs. The final piece should match the brief it came from.

items: LikeAnOpenSource (LAOS) is a platform for turning open-source curiosity into grounded understanding and useful contribution paths.
Instead of pretending a large catalog already exists, the platform is being built in public with a GitHub-backed backlog and a community-written publishing model.

items.label: Build in public
items.text: Let the repository show what the platform still needs, not only what it has already finished.

items.label: Prefer honest empty states
items.text: A clear backlog is more trustworthy than a fake catalog.

items.label: Make contribution feel possible
items.text: People should leave the site feeling closer to a useful contribution, not further from it.

items: Every visible topic or published piece should earn its place by solving a real editorial gap and helping the next reader make a better decision.

blocks.text: Trust is the product. If the site claims something exists, visitors should be able to see the real repository-backed source behind it.

items.title: Main repository
items.description: Track the codebase, the backlog, and the content model that powers the site.

items.title: Open contribution topics
items.description: Browse the live topic backlog that the site reads directly from the repository.
